Journal.Title: Australian critical care : official journal of the Confederation of Australian Critical Care Nurses

Article.ArticleTitle: Near-infrared spectroscopy (NIRS) during and after cardiopulmonary resuscitation: A systematic review and meta-analysis.

Abstract.AbstractText: Outcome prediction during and after cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) poses major challenges for prehospital and in-hospital emergency services. Regional cerebral oxygen saturation (rSO2) measured by near-infrared spectroscopy (NIRS), an emerging technology in emergency medicine, could contribute to prediction approaches. This systematic review evaluated associations between NIRS values during both cardiac arrest (CA) and postresuscitation care and clinical outcomes in nontraumatic adult CA patients.

We searched Embase, MEDLINE(R), Cochrane Central Register of Controlled Trials, and Web of Science. Data on study design, population characteristics, and different cerebral oximetry levels in each outcome group were extracted. Risk of bias was assessed using the Quality In Prognosis Studies tool, and a meta-analysis was conducted in groups with at least three studies reporting for the same outcome.

The search strategy identified 2055 records, and 48 studies with rSO2 measurements in 3880 patients during or after a cardiopulmonary resuscitation were included for data extraction. In total, we performed 10 different meta-analyses, with four evaluating rSO2 values during CA and six assessing rSO2 during postresuscitation care. Overall, delta rSO2 values during CA had the highest predictive power of return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC) (standardised mean difference = 1.61, 95% confidence interval = 0.57-2.66; p = 0.002). After ROSC, higher rSO2 values are associated with favourable neurological outcome (standardised mean difference = 0.38, 95% confidence interval = 0.12-0.65; p = 0.004).

Higher rSO2 values during CA are consistently associated with increased rates of ROSC, with delta rSO2 values having the highest predictive power. However, from a practical point of view, retrospectively calculating delta values poses a significant limitation which could be overcome by rather using real-time trends. Also, the implementation of NIRS in postresuscitation care could be an additional component of early multimodal prognostication after CA as we showed that higher initial rSO2 values after ROSC are associated with a favourable neurological outcome.

MedlineCitation.CoiStatement: Declaration of competing interests Christoph Veigl is member of the Young European Resuscitation Council committee. Sebastian Schnaubelt is a member of International Liaison Committee on Resuscitation Education, Implementation and Teams Task Force, and European Resuscitation Council Advanced Life Support Science and Education Committee and Vice-Chair of the Austrian Resuscitation Council.
